United Fruit Company · Steamship Service

The Great White Fleet

La Gran Flota Blanca

A catalogue of postcards, advertisements, and ephemera documenting the United Fruit Company's steamship line — the vessel that carried Costa Rica's bananas north and carried tourists, and a particular romantic idea of the tropics, south.

Costa Rica Histórica — Maritime & Commerce Series
  • Placa 01 — The Call of the Caribbean
  • Placa 02 — Loading-Bananas
  • Placa 03 — Limón Railway Pier
  • Placa 04 — The Banana Steamer
  • Placa 05 — 23 Day Cruise
  • Sailing Ports
Introducción · Introduction

Two Cargoes, One Hull

The Great White Fleet was the passenger-and-tourism arm of the United Fruit Company's shipping operation — the same hulls, painted white rather than the rust-red of the working freighters, that carried the company's executives, agricultural inspectors, and, increasingly through the 1910s and 1920s, paying vacationers between New York, New Orleans, and the company's banana ports across the Caribbean basin and Central America's Atlantic coast, Costa Rica's Puerto Limón chief among them.

This was never simply a shipping line. It was the maritime artery of the banana economy itself — and, at the same moment, a vehicle for selling the tropics back to North America as a destination of moonlit decks and "radiant seas." The same company, the same route map, the same wharves at Limón, produced two entirely different kinds of literature. This catalogue holds both.

The Brochure Voice

Glossy magazine advertising sold the Fleet as romance: moonlight on deck, a lady's fur collar, the "purple of tropic palms." Aimed at the leisure traveler with money and sixteen to twenty-four days to spend.

The Working Voice

The same ships, the same company, photographed at anchor with smoke pouring from the stack and a hold full of fruit — the unglamorous reality of an agro-export economy that the brochures never pictured.

Placa 01
December 1916

The Call of the CaribbeanLa Llamada del Caribe

Source
Country Life in America, December 1916, page 85
Publisher
United Fruit Co. Steamship Service, 17 Battery Place, New York
Format
Full-page color magazine advertisement
Subject
Moonlit shipboard vignette; Caribbean & Central American route map; toucan vignette

The composition does the company's marketing work in two registers at once. Above, a vignette of evening on deck — a string quartet of fellow passengers, a couple wrapped in furs against the sea air, a full moon over the rail — sells the Fleet as floating high society. Below, a route map fans out from New York and New Orleans through Cuba, Jamaica, Belize, Guatemala, and down the Central American coast — Puntarenas, San José, and Limón named directly on the Costa Rican leg — toward Panama and on to Colombia.

El anuncio combina dos registros: el romance de la cubierta nocturna y la geografía precisa de las rutas comerciales de la compañía.

The text promises sailings "every Wednesday and Saturday" from New York, a fixed schedule that betrays the operation's real nature: this was freight logistics with a passenger cabin bolted on, not a cruise line that happened to also move fruit.

Cuba Jamaica Panama Canal Puntarenas San José Limón Colombia
Placa 02
Real-photo postcard, early 20th c.

Loading-BananasCargando Bananos

Caption
"Loading-Bananas"
Format
Black-and-white real-photo postcard
Publisher
Unrecorded — verso details pending review
Subject
UFCo steamer alongside wharf; banana loading conveyor; rail car lettered "890 Northern" at left

Where Placa 01 staged the Fleet as theatre and the postcard at Placa 04 shows the ship simply at anchor, this card goes inside the machinery of the transfer itself. An angled conveyor chute runs from the dockside warehouse down to the ship's hold, carrying loaded stems of bananas across the gap between rail and hull. Dock workers cluster at left, and a boxcar lettered "890 NORTHERN" sits on the wharf track — a direct, legible link to the Atlantic-slope rail network that fed the port.

This is the most mechanically explicit image in the catalogue so far: it shows, rather than states, how a banana economy actually moved its product — rail car to wharf to conveyor to hold, all in one frame, with no romance in any direction.

La fotografía documenta el mecanismo concreto de transferencia: del vagón de ferrocarril al muelle, de la rampa al casco del vapor.

Banana trade Northern Railway Wharf operations
Placa 03
Postcard, early 20th c.

Limón, Railway Pier C.R.Limón, Muelle del Ferrocarril

Caption
"Limón, Railway Pier C. R."
Format
Black-and-white postcard, photo-litho
Publisher
Unrecorded — verso details pending review
Subject
Wide harbor view, railway pier with switch-houses, steamers berthed, passenger rail car in foreground

This is the establishing shot the rest of the catalogue has been pointing toward: the pier itself, reaching out into Limón's harbor with two switch-houses standing on the track bed and at least two steamers berthed along its length. A sailboat cuts across the still water behind, and a passenger railcar sits in the foreground on the near track — the same Northern Railway whose rolling stock appears, lettered, in Placa 02.

Where Placa 01's route map names Limón only by implication and the working cards show fragments of the transfer process, this card supplies the geography: the actual pier, the actual harbor, the physical point where Costa Rica's rail network met the Fleet's hulls. It resolves the "implicit" note left against Puerto Limón in this catalogue's port ledger.

Esta vista general del muelle ferroviario de Limón es la imagen que sitúa geográficamente a todas las demás placas de este catálogo.

Puerto Limón Northern Railway Railway pier
Placa 04
Real-photo postcard, early 20th c.

The Banana SteamerEl Vapor Bananero

Caption
"U. F. Co's Steamer carrying Bananas to U. S. A."
Format
Black-and-white real-photo postcard
Publisher
Unrecorded — verso details pending review
Subject
UFCo freighter at anchor, single funnel under steam, twin masts

No moonlight here. The caption states the cargo plainly, and the photograph obliges: a loaded freighter sits at anchor, dark smoke pouring from its funnel, the far shore reduced to a flat line of hills behind still water. Figures are visible on deck, but the ship itself — not the passengers — is the subject.

This is the counterweight to Placa 01's deck-party romance: the same fleet, the same company initials, doing the work the brochures were selling against. Where Placa 01 names Limón only as a stop on a pleasure cruise, this card is a reminder of why the route existed in the first place.

El contraste con la Placa 01 es deliberado: el mismo buque, la misma compañía, dos propósitos completamente distintos.

Banana trade UFCo fleet
Placa 05
c. 1920s

23 Day All-Expense CruiseCrucero Todo Incluido de 23 Días

Offer
23-day all-expense cruise: Cuba, Jamaica, Panama, Costa Rica
Publisher
United Fruit Company — Room 1645, 17 Battery Place, New York; General Offices, 131 State St., Boston
Premium
Free booklet, "The Gates of the Caribbean," by William McFee
Logo
Great White Fleet diamond housemark

By the time of this advertisement the pitch has matured from atmosphere into itinerary. The copy itemizes the package down to the day: an automobile tour of Havana, a special train to Hershey, a sixty-mile motor crossing of Jamaica, a Panama Canal Zone tour with lunch at the Hotel Tivoli, and — the Costa Rican leg — a mountain railway journey to San José with hotel accommodation included.

The Columbus landing-party vignette at lower right is pure marketing iconography, gesturing at four centuries of "discovery" romance with no connection to the modern itinerary above it. It sits oddly against the very concrete, very commercial offer: a fixed-price, fixed-schedule consumer product, complete with a give-away book to seal the sale.

El folleto promocional "The Gates of the Caribbean" de William McFee era el obsequio publicitario que acompañaba la oferta.

Havana Jamaica Panama Canal Zone San José

Costa Rican Ports of Call, as Named in This Catalogue

PortNamed InNote
PuntarenasPlaca 01Pacific-coast terminus named directly on the 1916 route map
San JoséPlacas 01 & 05Inland capital reached by rail from the Pacific coast; featured in both the 1916 map and the 1920s cruise itinerary
Puerto LimónPlacas 02 & 03The company's principal Atlantic banana port; named directly on the railway pier postcard (Placa 03) and shown at wharf level in the loading scene (Placa 02)

Catálogo en expansión

Five plates open this catalogue: a 1916 magazine advertisement, two real-photo postcards documenting the loading wharf and railway pier at Puerto Limón, a postcard of a working banana steamer, and a 1920s package-tour advertisement. Further Great White Fleet material — additional sailings advertisements, ship postcards, company ephemera — will be added as it is acquired.
